steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Andromeda — home of the Andromeda Galaxy, the most distant object visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: Look high in the autumn sky
steps.caption: In October and November, Andromeda rides high overhead for northern observers. The Great Square of Pegasus is the key landmark — Andromeda attaches to the northeast corner of the Square at the star Alpheratz. From southern latitudes below about 40°S the constellation is not well seen.

steps.title: Three stars in a chain
steps.caption: Andromeda's backbone is a curving chain of three equally bright stars: Alpheratz, Mirach, and Almach. Almach is a beautiful colour-contrast double — orange and blue-green — easily split in a small telescope.

steps.title: The Andromeda Galaxy
steps.caption: The Andromeda Galaxy is the farthest object most people ever see with unaided eyes — 2.5 million light-years away. To find it, hop two stars north from Mirach. Under dark skies it appears as a large fuzzy oval, several times the diameter of the full Moon. It contains around a trillion stars and is approaching us at 110 km/s, set to merge with the Milky Way in about 4.5 billion years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Antlia — the Air Pump, one of the faintest constellations in the sky, invented by Lacaille in the 1750s and lying between Hydra and Vela in the southern spring sky.

steps.title: Between Hydra and Vela
steps.caption: Antlia lies between Hydra to the north and Vela to the south. From Alphard — the solitary orange star that marks Hydra's heart — drop about 25° due south to reach Antlia's sparse territory. It is best seen from February to April and is invisible north of about 45°N.

steps.title: Three faint stars
steps.caption: Antlia's three brightest stars — Alpha (mag 4.3), Epsilon (mag 4.5), and Theta (mag 4.6) — are all barely visible to the naked eye and form no recognizable shape. Alpha Antliae is an orange giant about 370 light-years away. There is no useful asterism; sweep the region between Alphard and the Vela star fields with binoculars.

steps.title: Lacaille's air pump
steps.caption: Antlia honors the air pump of experimental physics — one of 14 instrument constellations Lacaille invented after his 1751–52 Cape of Good Hope survey. The region contains the spiral galaxy NGC 2997, a face-on spiral about 40 million light-years away visible in a small telescope from dark southern sites.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Apus — the Bird of Paradise, a small far-southern constellation between Triangulum Australe and the south celestial pole.

steps.title: South of Triangulum Australe
steps.caption: Apus lies about 20° south of Triangulum Australe — the compact bright southern triangle. Find Atria (Alpha TrA, mag 1.9), the brightest and southernmost vertex of that triangle, then sweep south into the sparser region below it. Apus is best seen May–July from southern latitudes and is invisible north of about 25°N.

steps.title: A faint quadrilateral
steps.caption: Alpha Apodis (mag 3.8) is an orange-red giant and the brightest star in the constellation. The four main stars form a loose, undistinguished quadrilateral with no memorable shape. The constellation is circumpolar from most of Australia and southern South America.

steps.title: Bird of Paradise
steps.caption: Apus was introduced by the Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century, naming it after the exotic Birds of Paradise they encountered on their East Indies voyages. The constellation sits adjacent to Octans (which contains the south celestial pole) and Triangulum Australe, making it a useful region to learn for south polar navigation.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Aquarius — the Water Bearer, a sprawling but faint zodiacal constellation best known for its Y-shaped Water Jar asterism.

steps.title: Look south in autumn
steps.caption: In September and October, Aquarius crosses the southern meridian during the evening hours for northern observers, sitting between about 0° and -25° declination. It is an expansive but faint constellation — no stars brighter than magnitude 2.9 — so dark skies help considerably.

steps.title: Find the Water Jar
steps.caption: The Y-shaped Water Jar asterism sits northeast of Sadalsud, with Zeta Aquarii (a close double star) at its junction. Fomalhaut — the lone first-magnitude star about 30° to the south in Piscis Austrinus — is a useful anchor: from Fomalhaut, sweep north and the Water Jar floats above it.

steps.title: The water bearer's full extent
steps.caption: The constellation sprawls south and east of the Water Jar, with streams of stars representing water pouring from the jar toward Fomalhaut in Piscis Austrinus to the south. The Saturn Nebula (NGC 7009) near Nu Aquarii is a fine planetary nebula in small telescopes, and the Helix Nebula (NGC 7293) nearby is the closest planetary nebula to Earth at about 650 light-years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Aquila — the Eagle, a summer constellation whose heart is Altair, the southernmost star of the Summer Triangle.

steps.title: The summer Milky Way
steps.caption: Look to the southern sky on a summer evening. Aquila straddles the Milky Way on the celestial equator, visible from both hemispheres. In August it rides highest in the south for northern observers.

steps.title: Three stars in a line
steps.caption: Altair is easy to identify: a bright star flanked closely by a dimmer star on each side, all three nearly in a straight line. Tarazed (slightly orange, to the north) and Alshain (to the south) give Altair the impression of a bird with spread wings.

steps.title: The Eagle
steps.caption: Pull back to see the full eagle, wings spread along the Milky Way. Altair sits almost exactly on the celestial equator — it rises nearly due east and sets nearly due west from any location on Earth.

steps.title: Summer Triangle
steps.caption: Altair is the southernmost point of the Summer Triangle. Look northeast to Vega in Lyra, and north to Deneb in Cygnus. The Triangle spans a wide swath of the summer Milky Way and is the most prominent star pattern in the summer evening sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Ara — the Altar, a compact southern constellation with several bright stars just south of Scorpius's stinger.

steps.title: Below the scorpion's tail
steps.caption: Ara lies about 15° south of the scorpion's stinger stars. Find Shaula, the brightest of the tail stars, then drop due south — the compact rectangular outline of the Altar is right there. The Milky Way runs richly through this region, making it a spectacular binocular field.

steps.title: Find Beta and Alpha Arae
steps.caption: Beta Arae (mag 2.8, blue) and Alpha Arae (mag 2.8, orange-red) are the two brightest stars, nearly equal in brightness. Several other third-magnitude stars complete the altar's rectangular outline spanning about 10°. Ara is best seen June–August from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: The ancient altar
steps.caption: Ara is one of the 48 original Ptolemaic constellations — the altar upon which the Olympian gods swore oaths before the Titanomachy. The constellation contains NGC 6397, one of the nearest globular clusters to Earth at about 7,800 light-years, visible as a fuzzy star in binoculars. The dense Milky Way star fields through Ara reward any sweeping with binoculars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Aries — the Ram, a small but historically important zodiacal constellation that was once the location of the vernal equinox.

steps.title: Look west of the Pleiades
steps.caption: In November and December evenings, Aries is well-placed high in the southern sky for northern observers. The Pleiades star cluster (in Taurus) is the easiest landmark: look about 20° west of the Pleiades to find the small curved arc of Aries. The constellation has just a handful of notable stars.

steps.title: Find Hamal and Sheratan
steps.caption: Hamal is the constellation's brightest star — an orange giant about 66 light-years away. Sheratan, just 4° west of Hamal, forms an obvious pair with it. A third fainter star, Mesarthim, extends the arc.

steps.title: The First Point of Aries
steps.caption: Though Aries is small and modest, it was the most important constellation in ancient astronomy: the vernal equinox, where the Sun crosses the celestial equator each March, was once located here. Due to precession it has since moved into Pisces, but 'First Point of Aries' remains the term for the vernal equinox direction. Aries is best seen from mid-northern latitudes in November.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Auriga — the Charioteer, anchored by brilliant Capella, the sixth-brightest star in the sky and a golden-yellow beacon of the winter sky.

steps.title: Look high in the winter sky
steps.caption: In January and February, Capella passes nearly overhead at mid-northern latitudes, making it one of the most prominent winter stars. From Orion's belt, move north and slightly west for about 45° to reach Capella. Capella is circumpolar for observers north of about 44°N — it never sets from those latitudes.

steps.title: Find Capella
steps.caption: Capella appears as a single golden-yellow star but is actually two giant G-type stars orbiting each other every 104 days, about 43 light-years away. It is very similar in color to the Sun but far more luminous. Look for a prominent pentagon of stars with Capella at the top — that is Auriga.

steps.title: The pentagon and the Kids
steps.caption: Menkalinan marks the opposite corner of the pentagon from Capella. Three faint stars called the Kids — Epsilon, Zeta, and Eta Aurigae — cluster just southwest of Capella. Epsilon Aurigae is a remarkable eclipsing system: a supergiant star eclipsed every 27 years by a vast disk of unknown material.

steps.title: Rich in star clusters
steps.caption: The Milky Way passes through Auriga, and the constellation contains three fine Messier open clusters: M36, M37 (the richest, containing about 500 stars), and M38. All three are visible as hazy patches in binoculars and resolve into lovely star fields in a small telescope. They lie roughly along a line in the southern part of the pentagon.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Boötes — the Herdsman, home to Arcturus, the brightest star north of the celestial equator and a brilliant orange beacon of the spring sky.

steps.title: Arc to Arcturus
steps.caption: In April and May evenings, Boötes is high in the southeast sky. The classic mnemonic 'Arc to Arcturus' guides you straight there: follow the curved arc of the Big Dipper's handle outward for about 30° and you land on Arcturus. This is one of the most reliable star-hops in astronomy.

steps.title: Find Arcturus
steps.caption: Arcturus is unmistakably orange — an evolved K-type giant, about 37 light-years away, and the fourth-brightest star in the night sky. It is moving through space at an unusually high velocity relative to the Sun and belongs to the thick disk of the Milky Way, older stars with a different orbital history.

steps.title: The kite shape of Boötes
steps.caption: Boötes forms a kite or ice cream cone shape with Arcturus at its tip (southernmost point). Izar marks one side of the kite — it is a stunning orange-and-blue double star in a small telescope, nicknamed Pulcherrima ('most beautiful') by astronomers. The kite widens north from Arcturus toward a line of four stars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Caelum — the Chisel, one of the smallest and faintest constellations, invented by Lacaille and tucked south of Eridanus between Columba and Horologium.

steps.title: South of Lepus, east of Columba
steps.caption: Caelum lies roughly 30° east-southeast of Rigel in Orion, south of the river Eridanus and east of Columba the Dove. There is no bright anchor star nearby — the best approach is to find Rigel and sweep southeast into the sparse southern field. It is best seen November–January and is invisible north of 40°N.

steps.title: A tiny pair of faint stars
steps.caption: Alpha Caeli (mag 4.4) is the brightest star, a white subgiant about 66 light-years away. Beta Caeli (mag 5.0) and Gamma (mag 4.4) are the other main stars. There is no recognizable pattern — Caelum is simply a faint wisp of a constellation in an empty corner of the southern sky.

steps.title: Lacaille's engraving tool
steps.caption: Caelum is one of 14 instrument constellations Lacaille introduced after his 1751–52 Cape of Good Hope survey, honoring the burin — the engraving tool used by printmakers and sculptors. It is among the most challenging constellations to identify and is routinely skipped by beginners. With no deep-sky objects of note, it is purely a completionist's target.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Camelopardalis — the Giraffe, a very large but very faint circumpolar constellation filling the gap between Polaris and the bright stars of Perseus and Cassiopeia.

steps.title: Between Polaris and Perseus
steps.caption: Camelopardalis sprawls between Polaris to the north and Perseus/Auriga to the south. The best strategy is to identify Cassiopeia's W to the east and Perseus to the south, then note the large, empty-looking region between them and Polaris — that is the Giraffe. It is circumpolar from most of the northern hemisphere and best seen on winter evenings.

steps.title: Beta and Alpha Camelopardalis
steps.caption: Beta Camelopardalis (mag 4.0) is the brightest star — a yellow supergiant about 1,000 light-years away. Alpha Cam (mag 4.3) is a distant blue supergiant. Despite the faintness, the constellation spans about 60° from top to bottom. Sweep slowly with binoculars to trace the meandering chain of 4th-magnitude stars.

steps.title: The forgotten northern giant
steps.caption: Camelopardalis was introduced in 1613 by Petrus Plancius, representing the giraffe that carried Rebekah to Isaac. Despite its enormous area (3rd largest constellation), beginners often miss it entirely because it lacks any star above magnitude 4. It contains NGC 2403, one of the nearest grand-design spiral galaxies at about 8 million light-years — a fine binocular target from dark sites.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Cancer — the Crab, the faintest of the zodiacal constellations, but home to the beautiful Beehive Cluster, a naked-eye star cluster known since antiquity.

steps.title: Look between Gemini and Leo
steps.caption: In February and March evenings, Cancer is due south at midnight. It lies between the unmistakable twin stars of Gemini (Pollux and Castor) to the west and bright Regulus in Leo to the east — draw a line between them and Cancer occupies the middle third. Its stars are all below magnitude 3.5, so this region will look conspicuously sparse.

steps.title: Spot the Beehive Cluster
steps.caption: The Beehive is a naked-eye fuzzy patch at the center of Cancer, visible as a smear of light under dark skies and resolving in binoculars into dozens of stars. It is one of the nearest open clusters at about 610 light-years. The faint Y-shape of Cancer's main stars surrounds the cluster on four sides.

steps.title: Cancer's main stars
steps.caption: Acubens and Asellus Australis are the most identifiable stars, flanking the Beehive. Asellus Australis and Asellus Borealis are the Southern and Northern Donkeys of ancient lore, watching over the Beehive manger. Cancer's inconspicuous stars make it an ideal dark-sky test: if you can see the individual Beehive stars naked-eye, conditions are excellent.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Canes Venatici — the Hunting Dogs, a small spring constellation below the handle of the Big Dipper, anchored by the bright blue-white star Cor Caroli.

steps.title: Below the Big Dipper's handle
steps.caption: Find the Big Dipper's handle — the three stars arcing away from the bowl. From Alkaid, the star at the very end of the handle, drop about 20° due south and you land directly on Cor Caroli. This is the brightest star in the Hunting Dogs and one of the easiest star-hops in the spring sky.

steps.title: Cor Caroli
steps.caption: Cor Caroli ("Charles's Heart", mag 2.9) is a mild magnetic variable and an easy, attractive double star in a small telescope. Chara (mag 4.3) lies about 3.5° to the southwest. Despite having only two named stars, the constellation is rich in background galaxies including M51 (the Whirlpool) and the globular cluster M3.

steps.title: Galaxy country
steps.caption: Canes Venatici contains some of the finest galaxies in the sky: M51 (the Whirlpool Galaxy) near Alkaid, M63 (the Sunflower), M94, and M106. The globular cluster M3 is one of the finest in the northern sky at magnitude 6.2 — visible in binoculars as a fuzzy star. The constellation sits far from the Milky Way plane, giving unobstructed views of the deep universe.
steps.notes: highlight M51, M63, M94, M106, M3

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Canis Major — the Great Dog, home of Sirius, the brightest star in the entire night sky.

steps.title: The winter sky
steps.caption: Look to the southern sky on a winter evening. Canis Major follows Orion like a faithful hunting dog, sitting south and east of the Hunter. Sirius blazes so brightly it cannot be mistaken for anything else.

steps.title: Follow the Belt
steps.caption: Take Orion's three belt stars and follow their line southeast about 20°. You will arrive at Sirius — a brilliant blue-white star that outshines everything else in the night sky. At magnitude −1.4 it is nearly twice as bright as the second-brightest star, Canopus.

steps.title: The Great Dog
steps.caption: Sirius marks the dog's shoulder. Fainter stars trace the body, hindquarters and legs below. Adhara at the hindquarters is intrinsically far more luminous than Sirius — it simply appears dimmer because it is nearly 30 times further away.

steps.title: The Dog Star
steps.caption: Sirius is only 8.6 light-years away — our fifth-closest stellar neighbour — which explains much of its brilliance. The ancient Egyptians timed the annual Nile flood by its first dawn rising and called it Sopdet.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Canis Minor — the Lesser Dog, a tiny constellation defined almost entirely by one brilliant star: Procyon, the eighth-brightest star in the night sky.

steps.title: Look east of Orion in winter
steps.caption: In January and February, Procyon is bright and obvious in the southeast after Orion rises. From Betelgeuse at Orion's shoulder, move east-northeast about 25° and you will land directly on Procyon. The constellation is best seen January through March from both hemispheres.

steps.title: The Winter Triangle
steps.caption: Procyon forms the Winter Triangle with Sirius (Canis Major) and Betelgeuse (Orion) — a large asterism spanning about 25° per side. Procyon (mag 0.4) is a white star about 11.5 light-years away, one of the Sun's closest neighbors. Like Sirius, it has a white dwarf companion detectable only with large telescopes.

steps.title: The two-star constellation
steps.caption: Canis Minor contains only two stars bright enough to see without effort: Procyon and Gomeisa (Beta CMi, mag 2.9), a blue-white star about 3° to the northwest. The 'dog body' is largely imaginary — there is little else to see.

steps.title: A midpoint pointer
steps.caption: Canis Minor marks the midpoint between Gemini's Pollux and Sirius in Canis Major — a handy reference for navigating the winter sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Capricornus — the Sea Goat, a faint but distinctively shaped zodiacal constellation resembling an arrowhead or a bikini top in the southern autumn sky.

steps.title: Look south in autumn
steps.caption: In September and October evenings, Capricornus sits due south and relatively low in the sky from mid-northern latitudes. Start at Altair in Aquila and follow the direction of the eagle's beak. Capricornus lies in the relatively starless region between Sagittarius to the west and Aquarius to the east.

steps.title: Find Algedi and Dabih
steps.caption: The western tip of the arrowhead is marked by Algedi and Dabih, a pair of stars about 1° apart that form an optical double visible to the naked eye. Dabih is a genuine multiple star system. Together they define the 'head' end of the sea goat's triangular shape.

steps.title: The sea goat's outline
steps.caption: Deneb Algedi at the eastern tip is the constellation's brightest star — an eclipsing binary that dims slightly every 24.5 hours. The full arrowhead outline contains about eight to nine stars visible to the naked eye. Neptune was discovered in Capricornus in 1846.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Carina — the Keel, the brightest section of the ancient ship Argo, containing Canopus, the second-brightest star in the entire sky.

steps.title: Follow south from Sirius to Canopus
steps.caption: Drop about 35° due south of Sirius — the brightest star in the sky — and you arrive at brilliant Canopus, the unmistakable anchor of Carina. From southern latitudes Canopus is impossible to miss; from below 30°N it just grazes the southern horizon. The constellation rides the southern Milky Way in spectacular star fields.

steps.title: Four bright stars
steps.caption: Carina contains a remarkable collection of bright stars: Canopus (mag −0.7), Avior (mag 1.9, orange), Alsephina (mag 1.9, blue), and Aspidiske (mag 2.2, blue). These four, plus two bright stars of Vela, form the False Cross — an asterism sometimes mistaken for the true Southern Cross.
steps.notes: highlight Alsephina; is the false cross really 6 stars?; Aspidiske label covers Avior

steps.title: Eta Carinae Nebula
steps.caption: The Eta Carinae Nebula (NGC 3372) is one of the largest and most active star-forming regions in the Milky Way, visible to the naked eye as a bright patch. The hypergiant Eta Carinae within it is one of the most massive and unstable stars known — a candidate for a future supernova. The Southern Pleiades (IC 2602) nearby is one of the finest open clusters in the sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Cassiopeia — a brilliant W-shape high in the northern sky, circumpolar and visible year-round from most northern latitudes.

steps.title: Look north
steps.caption: Turn to the northern sky. Cassiopeia wheels around the celestial pole without ever setting. When the Big Dipper is low, Cassiopeia is high, and vice versa — they sit on opposite sides of the pole. In autumn evenings it passes nearly overhead.

steps.title: The W shape
steps.caption: Five second-magnitude stars form an unmistakable W — or M, depending on its orientation as it circles the pole. No other pattern in the northern sky has this zigzag. Schedar (orange) and Caph anchor the outer ends; Cih sits at the sharp central peak.

steps.title: Rich in the Milky Way
steps.caption: Cassiopeia sits embedded in the brightest part of the northern Milky Way. The W is an excellent guide to this star-rich region — sweep it with binoculars for open clusters and nebulae. It is one of the most rewarding fields in the northern sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Centaurus — the Centaur, a spectacular southern constellation containing the two nearest star systems to Earth: Alpha Centauri and Proxima Centauri.

steps.title: Look south in the southern hemisphere
steps.caption: Centaurus is best seen from the southern hemisphere in April and May, when it culminates nearly overhead from latitudes around -35°. From northern latitudes above 25°N, only the northern edge is visible. Look for two unmistakably brilliant stars, Alpha and Beta Centauri, low in the south from subtropical northern locations.

steps.title: The Southern Pointers
steps.caption: Rigil Kentaurus (Alpha Centauri) is the third-brightest star in the night sky and the nearest star system to the Sun at 4.24 light-years — it is actually a triple system including dim Proxima Centauri, the closest individual star. Hadar is 4° away. Together they are called the Southern Pointers because they point directly to the Southern Cross (Crux).

steps.title: The full centaur
steps.caption: The Centaur's body sprawls over a large area north of Alpha and Beta Centauri, with Menkent marking the head region. The constellation is the ninth-largest in the sky. Omega Centauri (NGC 5139), near the northern boundary, is the largest and most massive globular cluster in the Milky Way — nearly 18 million solar masses in a sphere 150 light-years across, visible to the naked eye as a fuzzy star.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Cepheus — the King, a circumpolar northern constellation shaped like a child's drawing of a house, sitting between Cassiopeia and Polaris.

steps.title: Look near the north celestial pole
steps.caption: Cepheus is circumpolar for observers north of about 35°N and is best positioned on autumn evenings when it rides high overhead near the zenith. Find Cassiopeia's distinctive W shape first, then look on its northern side — Cepheus forms a blunt pentagon between Cassiopeia and Polaris.

steps.title: The house-shaped asterism
steps.caption: The bright star Alderamin marks one corner of the house's base. The triangular 'roof' points north toward Polaris. Although no star stands out dramatically, the house shape — once learned — is easy to recognize in a circumpolar sweep.

steps.title: Garnet Star and Delta Cephei
steps.caption: Mu Cephei, the Garnet Star (not in the main line set), is one of the largest and reddest stars visible to the naked eye — a red supergiant. Delta Cephei is the prototype Cepheid variable, pulsating with a 5.4-day period that is perfectly predictable. Astronomers use Cepheids like Delta Cephei as 'standard candles' to measure cosmic distances.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Cetus — the Whale, one of the largest constellations in the sky, sprawling across the autumn equatorial sky between Pisces and Eridanus.

steps.title: South of Pisces, west of Eridanus
steps.caption: Cetus has two distinct parts: the head (north) marked by Menkar (Alpha Ceti, mag 2.5), and the body (south) marked by Diphda (Beta Ceti, mag 2.0), the constellation's brightest star. Find Diphda about 35° south of Alpheratz (the top-left corner of Pegasus's Great Square). The two groups connect via a curving chain of faint stars.
steps.notes: text is redundant with next slide; this should just talk about locating; save stars for next

steps.title: Diphda and Menkar
steps.caption: Diphda (Beta Ceti, mag 2.0) is the brightest star, an orange giant about 96 light-years away. Menkar (Alpha Ceti, mag 2.5) is a red giant marking the monster's open jaw. Between them stretches a chain of 4th-magnitude stars — recognizable from dark sites but hard to trace under suburban skies.

steps.title: Mira the Wonderful
steps.caption: Mira (Omicron Ceti) is one of the sky's most famous variable stars, pulsating between magnitude 2 and magnitude 10 over about 332 days. At maximum it outshines every other star in Cetus; at minimum it vanishes without a telescope. Tau Ceti (magnitude 3.5, just 12 light-years away) is one of the nearest Sun-like stars and a prime target in the search for exoplanets.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Chamaeleon — the Chameleon, a small faint far-southern constellation near the south celestial pole, south of Carina.

steps.title: South of the False Cross
steps.caption: Chamaeleon lies between Carina to the north and Octans (the south pole) to the south, about 40° from the south celestial pole. Identify the False Cross in Carina/Vela, then sweep about 30° due south into increasingly sparse territory. It is circumpolar from latitudes south of 20°S and invisible north of 20°N.

steps.title: A loose kite of faint stars
steps.caption: Alpha Chamaeleontis (mag 4.1) is a white giant about 63 light-years away. The four brightest stars (magnitudes 4.1–4.5) form a loose kite or box shape spanning about 10°. None is particularly conspicuous — dark skies are needed to trace the pattern.

steps.title: A stellar nursery
steps.caption: Chamaeleon was introduced by the Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman around 1598. The constellation hosts the Chamaeleon Cloud complex, a star-forming region about 520 light-years away and one of the closest active stellar nurseries to Earth — heavily studied by infrared astronomers hunting newly-formed protostars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Circinus — the Drawing Compass, one of the smallest constellations in the sky, wedged just 4° from brilliant Alpha Centauri.

steps.title: Next to Alpha Centauri
steps.caption: Circinus sits right beside Alpha Centauri — one of the two bright Southern Pointers. Locate Alpha Centauri (the brighter of the two pointer stars) and look about 4° south-southeast. Alpha Circini (mag 3.2) is easily found in that direction. Best seen April–June from the southern hemisphere; invisible north of about 20°N.

steps.title: Alpha Circini
steps.caption: Alpha Circini (mag 3.2) is the only star worth noting — a blue-white star about 54 light-years away. Beta and Gamma Circini (both around mag 4.5) complete the tiny compass shape spanning just 5°. The entire constellation fits within a single binocular field.

steps.title: The Circinus Galaxy
steps.caption: Despite its obscurity, Circinus contains a remarkable deep-sky object: the Circinus Galaxy, one of the nearest Seyfert 2 galaxies at about 13 million light-years, heavily obscured by the Milky Way. It is one of the most active known galactic nuclei and is extensively studied in X-ray and radio wavelengths, though it requires a telescope to detect visually.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Columba — the Dove, a small constellation south of Lepus the Hare, anchored by the blue-white star Phact.

steps.title: South of Orion and Lepus
steps.caption: From Rigel in Orion, move about 20° south-southwest to find Phact, the brightest star in this part of the sky south of Orion. Alternatively, from Arneb at the head of the Hare, continue about 15° further south. Columba is best seen December–February.

steps.title: Phact and Wazn
steps.caption: Phact (mag 2.6) is a blue-white subgiant about 270 light-years away. Wazn (mag 3.1) is an orange giant about 87 light-years away. Together they mark the main body of the Dove, which stretches about 10° across.

steps.title: Noah's dove
steps.caption: Columba was introduced by Petrus Plancius in the 16th century, likely representing Noah's dove returning with an olive branch. The star Mu Columbae is a runaway star — a blue giant hurled from the Orion OB1 association at over 100 km/s by a supernova explosion millions of years ago, and now racing away from Orion through this region.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Coma Berenices — Berenice's Hair, a large sparse constellation whose stars form a glittering open cluster visible to the naked eye from dark sites.

steps.title: Between Leo and Boötes
steps.caption: Coma Berenices lies between Leo to the southwest and Boötes to the east. From Denebola (the tail of Leo), move about 15° north — the faint but wide scatter of Coma's stars will be there. From a truly dark site the Coma Star Cluster looks like a large, loose, fuzzy glow spanning several degrees.

steps.title: The Coma Star Cluster
steps.caption: The Coma Star Cluster (Melotte 111) contains about 40 stars spread across 5° of sky at a distance of 290 light-years. In binoculars it resolves into dozens of faint stars against a dark background — one of the sky's finest binocular clusters. Diadem (mag 4.3) marks the cluster's southern edge.

steps.title: Galaxy country
steps.caption: Behind the nearby star cluster lies the Coma Galaxy Cluster (Abell 1656), over a thousand galaxies some 330 million light-years away. The entire region around the North Galactic Pole falls in Coma Berenices, which is why the area is so extraordinarily rich in background galaxies for telescope observers. The Virgo Cluster's northern extent also spills into the southern edge of this constellation.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Corona Australis — the Southern Crown, a small but delicate arc of stars just south of Sagittarius's Teapot.

steps.title: Below the Teapot
steps.caption: Corona Australis lies about 10° directly south of Kaus Australis, the bright star at the base of the Teapot. The southern crown's semicircular arc of five stars is fainter than its northern counterpart, but the curved shape is recognizable in dark skies just below the rich Milky Way of Sagittarius. Best seen June–August.

steps.title: A faint semicircle
steps.caption: The arc spans about 6° and curves from northwest to southeast. Alpha Coronae Australis (mag 4.1) is the brightest star. The five main stars range from magnitude 4.1 to 4.6 — fainter than the Northern Crown but similarly shaped. All five fit comfortably in a single binocular field.

steps.title: A stellar nursery
steps.caption: The NGC 6726–6729 complex is a beautiful reflection and emission nebula within Corona Australis, associated with the R CrA star-forming region about 420 light-years away — one of the nearest active stellar nurseries to Earth. In long-exposure photographs, the nebulae glow blue and gold around embedded young stars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Corona Borealis — the Northern Crown, one of the most charming small constellations: a perfect semicircle of stars resembling a jeweled crown or laurel wreath.

steps.title: Look east of Boötes in spring
steps.caption: In May, June, and July, Corona Borealis is well-placed high in the east or overhead for northern observers. Find brilliant orange Arcturus in Boötes, then look about 22° to the east-northeast. The crown's semicircle sits in a region otherwise lacking bright stars, making it stand out despite the stars being only magnitude 2–4.

steps.title: Find Alphecca — the gem in the crown
steps.caption: Alphecca is the brightest star in the crown and marks its center. It is a spectroscopic binary about 75 light-years away. The full arc of seven stars spans only about 10° and is one of the tightest, most recognizable small patterns in the sky — no other similar curved arc exists nearby.

steps.title: A crown of seven stars
steps.caption: The seven stars of the crown arc from northwest to southeast, with Alphecca as the brightest 'gem.' T Coronae Borealis (the Blaze Star) is a recurrent nova lurking in the crown — it is normally at magnitude 10 but erupts to naked-eye brightness about every 80 years. The next eruption was predicted for around 2024–2025, so it may be visible as an extra bright star in this arc.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Corvus — the Crow, a compact, easy-to-recognize quadrilateral of four second-magnitude stars in the southern spring sky.

steps.title: Look southwest of Spica
steps.caption: In April and May evenings, Corvus is well-placed in the southern sky. Follow the classic pointer: 'Arc to Arcturus, speed on to Spica' — the mnemonic for following the Big Dipper's handle. Once you find brilliant blue-white Spica in Virgo, look about 15° to the southwest and you will see the compact tilted quadrilateral of Corvus.

steps.title: The compact crow
steps.caption: The four main stars — Gienah (mag 2.6), Kraz (mag 2.6), Algorab (mag 2.9), and Minkar — form a tight quadrilateral about 6° across. Gienah, the brightest, is a blue-white giant about 165 light-years away. A fifth star, Alchiba (mag 4.0), sits off one corner.

steps.title: The crow and the cup
steps.caption: Corvus and the neighboring constellation Crater (the Cup) both sit on the back of Hydra the Water Snake, which stretches across this part of the sky. The compactness and uniform brightness of Corvus's four main stars make it easy to identify once you know where to look — scan the area southwest of Spica and the neat quadrilateral jumps out.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Crater — the Cup, a small faint constellation sitting on the back of Hydra, just west of the more prominent quadrilateral of Corvus.

steps.title: West of Corvus on Hydra's back
steps.caption: Find the compact quadrilateral of Corvus — the crow southwest of Spica — then move about 15° to its west. Crater's circular ring of faint stars sits there on Hydra's back. The constellation is best seen March–May but is low in the south for northern observers above 40°N.

steps.title: Alkes and Delta Crateris
steps.caption: Delta Crateris (mag 3.6) is the brightest star, an orange giant about 163 light-years away. Alpha Crateris (Alkes, mag 4.1) marks the southern base of the cup. The eight main stars range from magnitude 3.6 to 5.2, forming a rough circular cup shape about 10° across.

steps.title: The cup, the crow, and the snake
steps.caption: In mythology, Apollo sent the crow to fetch water in the cup, carried on Hydra the Water Snake. The bird tarried; Apollo placed all three in the sky as punishment — which is why Crater, Corvus, and Hydra are neighbors to this day. The cup's circular outline, once learned, is Crater's most distinctive identifying feature.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Crux — the Southern Cross, the smallest constellation in the sky and the most iconic in the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: Look south
steps.caption: Crux is only visible from latitudes south of about 25°N — the further south you are, the higher it rides. In the southern hemisphere it is circumpolar, never setting. Look for a bright, compact kite of four stars low in the south from subtropical northern regions.

steps.title: The Southern Cross
steps.caption: Four bright stars form the cross: Acrux at the southern foot is the brightest, Gacrux at the top glows distinctly red, and Mimosa marks the left arm. The whole cross fits within a circle just 6° wide.

steps.title: Beware the False Cross
steps.caption: To the northwest lies the False Cross — four stars in Vela and Carina that form a similar pattern but are larger and less symmetrical. If your cross looks large and lopsided, you've found the impostor.

steps.title: Finding south
steps.caption: The long axis — from Gacrux through Acrux — points toward the south celestial pole. Extend it about 4.5 times its own length beyond Acrux and you arrive near the pole. Unlike the north, there is no bright star marking it.

steps.title: The Coalsack
steps.caption: Adjacent to Crux lies the Coalsack — a dark nebula, a patch conspicuously empty of stars where interstellar dust blocks the Milky Way behind it. Indigenous Australians saw it as a celestial emu's head, part of a sky mythology drawn in dark shapes rather than star patterns.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Cygnus — the Swan, whose stars form the unmistakable Northern Cross, one of the most striking asterisms of the summer Milky Way.

steps.title: Look overhead in summer
steps.caption: On a summer evening, look nearly overhead from mid-northern latitudes. Cygnus rides high through the Milky Way in August and September, with the Northern Cross oriented nearly vertical in the sky. From the southern hemisphere the cross appears low in the northern sky.

steps.title: Find Deneb and the Northern Cross
steps.caption: The brightest star, Deneb (mag 1.2), anchors the top of the Northern Cross and marks the swan's tail. Despite appearing similar in brightness to nearby stars, Deneb is actually a distant supergiant — roughly 2,600 light-years away — making it one of the intrinsically most luminous stars in the sky.

steps.title: The swan in flight
steps.caption: Sadr (mag 2.2) sits at the center of the cross — the swan's chest — while Albireo (mag 3.0) marks the beak at the southern tip, one of the most celebrated double stars, appearing gold and blue in a small telescope. The wings of the swan stretch east and west along the Milky Way band.

steps.title: Part of the Summer Triangle
steps.caption: Deneb forms the Summer Triangle with Vega (in Lyra, 24° to the west-southwest) and Altair (in Aquila, about 38° to the south). The Milky Way runs directly through Cygnus, making it one of the richest binocular regions in the sky. From a dark site the Great Rift — a dark lane of dust — bisects the constellation lengthwise.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Delphinus — the Dolphin, one of the most charming small constellations, a tiny diamond of stars that genuinely resembles a leaping dolphin.

steps.title: Look east of Aquila in summer
steps.caption: In August and September evenings, Delphinus is well-placed in the southern sky for northern observers, sitting about 10° east-northeast of Altair in Aquila. The Summer Triangle's Altair is easy to spot; from there, the compact diamond of Delphinus is visible about two fist-widths to the northeast.

steps.title: Job's Coffin
steps.caption: The four-star diamond asterism is sometimes called 'Job's Coffin.' All four stars are close in brightness (magnitudes 3.6–4.3), making the diamond quite uniform. The fifth star forms the dolphin's tail beneath the diamond. The constellation's compact size — fitting within a 5° circle — makes it easy to see in a single binocular field.
steps.notes: what's the tail star's name really? If its brightness is same as the four stars just say so

steps.title: The reversed names
steps.caption: Sualocin and Rotanev have an unusual origin: they are the reversed Latin spelling of 'Nicolaus Venator' — Niccolò Cacciatore, an assistant at Palermo Observatory who covertly named the stars after himself in the 1814 star catalog. The dolphin's empty surroundings in this otherwise star-poor region make it stand out despite its faintness.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Dorado — the Swordfish, a southern constellation best known for containing most of the Large Magellanic Cloud, one of the nearest galaxies to the Milky Way.

steps.title: East of Canopus — find the LMC
steps.caption: From Canopus (the second-brightest star in the sky), move about 25° to the east-northeast. In dark skies from the southern hemisphere, the Large Magellanic Cloud — a detached patch of milky light, clearly visible to the naked eye — makes Dorado unmistakable. Best seen November–February; circumpolar south of 30°S.

steps.title: The Large Magellanic Cloud
steps.caption: The LMC spans about 7° in diameter and is the second-nearest galaxy to the Milky Way at about 160,000 light-years. Beta Doradus (mag 3.3) is the brightest naked-eye star in the constellation proper, a Cepheid variable. But the LMC completely dominates the field — its Tarantula Nebula (NGC 2070) is the largest known emission nebula.

steps.title: The Tarantula Nebula
steps.caption: The Tarantula Nebula (NGC 2070) within the LMC is so immense that if placed at the Orion Nebula's distance it would cast visible shadows on Earth. The LMC also hosted Supernova 1987A — the nearest supernova seen from Earth in nearly 400 years — whose glowing remnant is still expanding and brightening. This is the most spectacular extragalactic object visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Draco — the Dragon, a long winding constellation that coils around the north celestial pole between Ursa Major and Ursa Minor.

steps.title: Look circumpolar in the north
steps.caption: Draco is circumpolar for observers north of about 20°N and winds around Ursa Minor through the entire northern sky. The best seasons to trace its full length are spring and summer evenings. The most recognizable feature is the Dragon's Head — a quadrilateral of four stars about 25° north-northwest of Vega.

steps.title: Find the Dragon's Head
steps.caption: The Dragon's Head is a compact trapezoid of four stars with Eltanin (mag 2.2) as its brightest corner — a reddish-orange giant about 148 light-years away. The head quadrilateral spans about 6° and is the most distinctive feature of this otherwise winding constellation.

steps.title: The dragon's long body
steps.caption: From the head, the Dragon's body snakes north then west, curving around Ursa Minor. Thuban, deep in the body, was the pole star around 2700 BC when the pyramids were being built. The full dragon spans about 60° of sky from head to tail.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Equuleus — the Little Horse, the second-smallest constellation in the sky, a tiny diamond of four faint stars just west of Delphinus.

steps.title: Just west of Delphinus
steps.caption: Find Delphinus — the compact diamond of Job's Coffin, easily spotted about 10° east of Altair in Aquila. Then look about 10° to its west and you will find the small L of Equuleus. From Altair itself, the Little Horse lies about 9° to the east-northeast. Best seen August–September.

steps.title: Kitalpha
steps.caption: Kitalpha (mag 3.9) is the constellation's sole notable star — its name means "part of a horse" in Arabic. The other stars range from magnitude 4.5 to 4.7. The entire constellation fits within a circle just 4° across, making it smaller even than Crux.
steps.notes: Kitalpha = part of a horse?? The other 3 stars??

steps.title: The ancient foal
steps.caption: Equuleus is one of Ptolemy's original 48 constellations, recognized since antiquity as a horse's head with no attached mythology. Despite being tiny and unremarkable, it has been continuously catalogued for over 2,000 years. Its location in an empty stretch of sky means the little diamond stands out slightly more than its faint magnitudes would suggest.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Eridanus — the River, a vast winding constellation stretching from near Orion all the way to the brilliant star Achernar deep in the southern sky.

steps.title: Start near Orion
steps.caption: The northern end of Eridanus begins at Cursa, just north of Rigel in Orion. In winter evenings, trace the river southward — it meanders west then curls back southeast, becoming progressively fainter. Achernar, the river's end at declination -57°, is only visible south of about 33°N latitude.

steps.title: Find the river's source at Cursa
steps.caption: Cursa (mag 2.8) sits about 3° north-northwest of Rigel and marks where the river 'pours' from Orion's foot. The northern section of Eridanus consists of a chain of 4th-magnitude stars that meander west and south through a relatively barren region of sky. Follow the chain star by star as it winds away from Orion.

steps.title: Achernar — end of the river
steps.caption: Achernar, the ninth-brightest star in the sky, is a rapidly rotating blue-white star spinning so fast it bulges at its equator. From the southern hemisphere it is a prominent circumpolar beacon; from latitudes above 33°N it never rises.

steps.title: The full river
steps.caption: Eridanus is the sixth-largest constellation, impossible to see entirely from any single latitude. From mid-southern latitudes the river arches dramatically across the sky in summer evenings — one of the grandest sweeps any constellation makes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Fornax — the Furnace, a faint southern constellation tucked inside a large southward bend of the river Eridanus.

steps.title: Inside the Eridanus bend
steps.caption: Fornax lies in a region devoid of bright stars inside a large meander of Eridanus, about 30° south of Diphda, the brightest star in Cetus. Its brightest star, Alpha Fornacis, is the sole landmark. Best seen October–December; invisible north of 50°N.

steps.title: Alpha Fornacis
steps.caption: Alpha Fornacis (mag 3.9) is a wide binary system — two yellow-white stars separated enough to be resolved in a small telescope, about 46 light-years away.

steps.title: The Fornax Galaxy Cluster
steps.caption: Fornax contains the Fornax Galaxy Cluster (Abell S0373), one of the two nearest large galaxy clusters. NGC 1316 and NGC 1365 (the Great Barred Spiral) are spectacular members visible in backyard telescopes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Gemini — the Twins, a winter constellation crowned by the bright pair Castor and Pollux, one of the most recognisable double stars in the sky.

steps.title: The winter sky
steps.caption: Turn to the southern sky on a winter evening. Gemini lies northeast of Orion in the same brilliant winter star field, best placed in the south for northern observers in January and February.

steps.title: Two bright stars side by side
steps.caption: Castor and Pollux are the twin heads — two bright stars sitting just 4.5° apart. From Orion, follow the line from Rigel through Betelgeuse and beyond — you'll arrive between them.

steps.title: The twin figures
steps.caption: Below each head, a chain of fainter stars stretches south-southwest — the bodies of the twins, lying side by side. The two chains run nearly parallel, making the whole constellation look like two people walking together.

steps.title: The Twins
steps.caption: Pollux is the nearer twin at just 34 light-years, and its warm orange colour betrays its giant status. Castor, only 4.5° away, is actually six stars — three gravitationally bound pairs — all masquerading as one point of light.

steps.title: The Geminids
steps.caption: Gemini straddles the Milky Way and hosts the radiant of the Geminid meteor shower every December — the year's most reliable and prolific display, with up to 120 meteors per hour emanating from just above Castor.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Grus — the Crane, a southern constellation with two bright stars (Alnair and Tiaki) forming a distinctive kite shape, easy to spot in southern autumn skies.

steps.title: Southeast of Fomalhaut
steps.caption: From Fomalhaut — the solitary bright Autumn Star of Piscis Austrinus — move about 20° south-southeast to reach Alnair and Tiaki. Best seen August–October from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: Alnair and Tiaki
steps.caption: Alnair (mag 1.7) is a blue-white star about 101 light-years away. Tiaki (mag 2.1) is a cool red giant about 170 light-years away. Together they anchor the kite-shaped body of the crane, with a chain of stars extending north representing the bird's long neck.

steps.title: The Grus Quartet
steps.caption: Grus contains several interacting galaxy groups visible in small telescopes. The Grus Quartet (NGC 7552, 7582, 7590, 7599) is a set of four galaxies about 60 million light-years away, all visible in the same telescope field and showing signs of gravitational interaction.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Hercules — the Hero, a large summer constellation identified by the distinctive Keystone asterism and home to the finest globular cluster visible from the northern hemisphere.

steps.title: Look between Vega and Arcturus
steps.caption: In June and July, Hercules passes high overhead for mid-northern observers. Draw an imaginary line from Vega (in Lyra) to Arcturus (in Boötes) — the Keystone of Hercules lies about one-third of the way from Vega.

steps.title: Find the Keystone
steps.caption: The Keystone is a quadrilateral of four stars forming the body of the kneeling hero. Once identified, the Keystone is easy to return to.
steps.notes:: Kornephoros is not in the keystone

steps.title: M13 — the Great Globular
steps.caption: The Great Globular Cluster M13 sits on the western edge of the Keystone, visible to the naked eye under dark skies as a faint fuzzy star. It contains roughly 300,000 stars packed into a sphere about 145 light-years across, about 22,000 light-years away.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Horologium — the Pendulum Clock, a long, thin, very faint southern constellation stretching north-south between Eridanus and Reticulum.

steps.title: East of Achernar
steps.caption: Horologium stretches east-southeast of Achernar, the brilliant blue star at the end of the river Eridanus. Alpha Horologii is the only remotely conspicuous star, sitting near the constellation's northern tip. Best seen October–December from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: A thin strip of faint stars
steps.caption: Alpha Horologii (mag 3.9) is an orange giant about 117 light-years away. The remaining stars are at the limit of naked-eye visibility even from dark sites.

steps.title: Huygens' clock
steps.caption: Horologium was named by Lacaille after Christiaan Huygens's 17th-century invention of the pendulum clock, which revolutionized time-keeping and navigation.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Hydra — the Water Snake, winding across a vast stretch of the spring sky anchored by the bright lone star Alphard.

steps.title: Look south in spring
steps.caption: Hydra sprawls across the southern sky in March and April, south of Virgo and Leo. Its brightest star Alphard is the easiest entry point. From Procyon (in Canis Minor), drop about 20° south to find orange Alphard glowing alone in an otherwise starless region.

steps.title: Find Alphard
steps.caption: Alphard (Alpha Hydrae, mag 2.0) is so conspicuously alone in its part of the sky that its name means 'the solitary one' in Arabic. It is an orange giant about 177 light-years away. An alternative hop: extend a line from Castor through Pollux in Gemini southward about 40° and you land near Alphard.

steps.title: The head and the long tail
steps.caption: West of Alphard, a ring of six faint stars (magnitudes 3.4–4.5) forms Hydra's head. Eastward the snake's body continues for another 60°, ending near the border of Libra. The Crow and the Cup sit on Hydra's back.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Hydrus — the Lesser Water Snake, a southern triangle of stars between the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds, not to be confused with the much larger Hydra.

steps.title: Between the Magellanic Clouds
steps.caption: Hydrus forms a triangle between Achernar (the bright blue end of Eridanus) and the south pole region. Beta Hydri, the nearest bright star to the south celestial pole, is the anchor. Find it in the gap between the two Magellanic Clouds.
steps.notes: highlight the Magellanic clouds; placement of beta, or language of the caption, is wrong

steps.title: Our sun's future
steps.caption: Beta Hydri is old enough to have evolved slightly off the main sequence — making it a near-perfect preview of what our own Sun will look like in about 1.5 billion years. At just 24 light-years it is one of the most studied solar analogs.
steps.notes: alpha and beta locations seem reversed; only highlight beta, if it's really the sun-like one

steps.title: Our Sun's future
steps.caption: Hydrus was introduced by Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century to fill the southern sky between the Clouds.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Indus — the Indian, a faint southern constellation lying between Pavo and Grus in the southern autumn sky.

steps.title: Between Pavo and Grus
steps.caption: Indus lies between Pavo to the south-southwest and Grus to the east. From Alnair in Grus, move about 15° northwest to locate Alpha Indi, the constellation's most distinctive star. Best seen August–October.

steps.title: Alpha and Theta Indi
steps.caption: Alpha Indi (mag 3.1) is an orange giant about 98 light-years away. The constellation's five visible stars range from magnitude 3.1 to 4.4, with no distinctive shape.

steps.title: Epsilon Indi — a nearby neighbor
steps.caption: Indus was introduced by Keyser and de Houtman to represent indigenous peoples encountered on Dutch voyages. Epsilon Indi, one of the nearest Sun-like stars, hosts a pair of T-dwarf brown dwarfs discovered in 2003.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Lacerta — the Lizard, a small autumn constellation sandwiched between Cygnus to the west and Andromeda/Cassiopeia to the east.

steps.title: Between Cygnus and Andromeda
steps.caption: Lacerta fills the gap between Cygnus and the northern tip of Andromeda. Identify Deneb (the tail of Cygnus) and the W of Cassiopeia, then look for the faint zigzag of stars between them. Best seen August–October for northern observers.

steps.title: A meandering chain
steps.caption: Alpha Lacertae (mag 3.8) is a blue-white star about 102 light-years away and the constellation's brightest. The other stars form a meandering chain of about 8 stars embedded in a rich section of the autumn Milky Way.

steps.title: BL Lacertae objects
steps.caption: Lacerta gives its name to BL Lacertae objects — a class of violently variable active galactic nuclei whose jets point nearly straight at us. BL Lac itself was initially catalogued as a variable star before its extragalactic nature was discovered.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Leo — the Lion, a spring constellation with one of the sky's most distinctive head shapes, anchored by bright Regulus.

steps.title: Look south in spring
steps.caption: Turn to the southern sky on a spring evening. Leo is the most prominent constellation of the spring sky for northern observers, riding high in the south in March and April.

steps.title: The Sickle
steps.caption: The lion's head and mane form a backward question mark called the Sickle. Regulus — a hot blue-white star sitting almost exactly on the ecliptic — anchors the base of the hook. It is the brightest star in Leo and impossible to miss once you know the shape.

steps.title: The full Lion
steps.caption: The Sickle forms Leo's head; a right-angled triangle of stars to the east traces the haunches and tail. Denebola marks the tail tip. The whole figure does evoke a crouching lion facing west, sickle raised like a mane.

steps.title: Found by the Pointers
steps.caption: A useful trick: extend the Big Dipper's Pointer Stars southward — opposite the direction to Polaris — and you land on Regulus. The same two stars that find the North Star also find the Lion's heart.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Leo Minor — the Lesser Lion, a faint constellation wedged between the large Leo and the Big Dipper's bowl in the spring sky.

steps.title: Between Leo and Ursa Major
steps.caption: Leo Minor lies halfway between Regulus (the bright blue-white forepaw of Leo) and the Big Dipper's bowl. Praecipua is the sole easily visible star in the region. Best seen March–April.

steps.title: Filling the gap
steps.caption: Leo Minor was introduced by Hevelius in 1687 from stars he felt were too isolated to belong to Leo. It has no mythology. Despite its faintness, the constellation contains several background galaxies accessible in backyard telescopes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Lepus — the Hare, a small constellation that crouches directly beneath Orion's feet in the winter sky.

steps.title: Look below Orion
steps.caption: In January and February evenings, Orion dominates the southern sky for northern observers. This makes Lepus one of the easiest secondary constellations to find: just look under Orion's feet.

steps.title: Find Arneb and the main quad
steps.caption: Arneb (mag 2.6) is a luminous white supergiant — the brightest star of the Hare. Nihal (mag 2.8) is an orange giant paired with Arneb to form the most obvious two-star anchor.

steps.title: Mira
steps.caption: Hind's Crimson Star (R Leporis) is a famous Mira variable near the northwestern boundary — when near maximum brightness it is one of the deepest red stars visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Libra — the Scales, the only zodiacal constellation representing an inanimate object, sandwiched between Virgo and Scorpius along the ecliptic.

steps.title: Look east of Spica in spring
steps.caption: In May and June evenings, Libra is due south at midnight for northern observers. Start at brilliant blue-white Spica in Virgo and move east-southeast to find it. Its brightest stars, Zubenelgenubi and Zubeneschamali, form an obvious pair.

steps.title: The scorpion's old claws
steps.caption: Zubeneschamali is one of the few stars that has been consistently described as greenish by observers. Zubenelgenubi is a wide naked-eye double, easily split with binoculars into a white pair. Their Arabic names mean 'the northern claw' and 'the southern claw,' recalling a time when they were considered part of Scorpius.

steps.title: The scales of the zodiac
steps.caption: The full constellation outlines a rough balance, with fainter stars to the south completing the scale pans. Libra lies on the ecliptic; planets are frequently found passing through it.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Lupus — the Wolf, a moderately bright southern constellation adjacent to Centaurus, with several 2nd and 3rd magnitude stars in a rich Milky Way stretch.

steps.title: East of Centaurus
steps.caption: Lupus lies between Centaurus and Scorpius along the southern Milky Way. From Hadar move east-northeast to reach Alpha Lupi, the constellation's brightest star. Best seen April–June from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: Alpha and Eta Lupi
steps.caption: Alpha Lupi (mag 2.3) is a blue giant about 460 light-years away. The constellation has no single memorable shape but its brightest stars are genuinely bright, making it one of the more conspicuous southern constellations despite lacking a well-known asterism.

steps.title: Supernova 1006
steps.caption: A naked-eye supernova blazed in Lupus in 1006 AD — the brightest ever recorded, estimated at magnitude −7.5 and bright enough to cast shadows at night. Its expanding remnant SN 1006 is now a faint X-ray and radio shell.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Lynx — a long, faint northern constellation so named by Hevelius because only someone with the eyes of a lynx could see it, filling the gap between Auriga and Ursa Major.

steps.title: Between Capella and the Big Dipper
steps.caption: Lynx occupies the large, sparse region between bright Capella to the southwest and the Big Dipper to the northeast. Identify those two landmarks and scan the relatively empty stretch between them. Best seen January–March.

steps.title: Alpha Lyncis
steps.caption: Alpha Lyncis (mag 3.1) is a red-orange giant about 222 light-years away and the sole beacon of the constellation. The long chain requires very dark skies to trace completely.

steps.title: The Intergalactic Wanderer
steps.caption: NGC 2419, the so-called Intergalactic Wanderer, is a remote globular cluster about 300,000 light-years away — farther than the Magellanic Clouds — yet still a gravitationally bound member of the Milky Way's halo.
steps.notes: explainn the wanderer name; highlight the wanderer

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Lyra — a small constellation but home to Vega, the fifth-brightest star in the sky and the dominant beacon of summer nights.

steps.title: The summer sky
steps.caption: Look overhead on a summer evening. From mid-northern latitudes Vega passes nearly directly overhead in August — impossible to miss. In the southern hemisphere it skims the northern horizon in winter.

steps.title: The Summer Triangle
steps.caption: Vega is the brightest corner of the Summer Triangle — an asterism formed with Deneb in Cygnus and Altair in Aquila.

steps.title: The magnitude standard
steps.caption: Vega blazes brilliant blue-white at magnitude 0 (the magnitude scale uses it as a base value). It was the north pole star around 12,000 BCE and will be again around 13,700 CE.

steps.title: The Lyre
steps.caption: Just south of Vega, four fainter stars form a small parallelogram — the frame of the lyre. The Ring Nebula (M57), easily found in a small telescope, lies between the two southern stars of the parallelogram.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Mensa — the Table Mountain, the intrinsically faintest of all 88 constellations, notable mainly because the southern edge of the Large Magellanic Cloud overlaps it.

steps.title: South of Dorado, under the LMC
steps.caption: Mensa lies directly south of Dorado, and the southern portion of the LMC extends into it. All five of Mensa's naked-eye stars are below the threshold of most suburban observers. Best seen November–January; circumpolar south of 40°S.

steps.title: The faintest constellation
steps.caption: The constellation was named by Lacaille after Table Mountain near Cape Town, the flat-topped landmark overlooking his observing site during his 1751–52 southern sky survey.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Microscopium — the Microscope, a faint southern constellation south of Capricornus with no star brighter than magnitude 4.7.

steps.title: South of Capricornus
steps.caption: Microscopium lies about 20° south of the Capricornus arrowhead, or about 15° north of Alnair in Grus, in a rather empty patch of southern autumn sky. There is no distinctive asterism.

steps.title: Lacaille's microscope
steps.caption: Lacaille named this constellation after the compound microscope — the revolutionary instrument of 17th-century science.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Monoceros — the Unicorn, a faint constellation in the heart of the Winter Triangle, filling the rich Milky Way between Orion, Canis Major, and Canis Minor.

steps.title: Inside the Winter Triangle
steps.caption: Monoceros fills the interior of the Winter Triangle formed by Sirius, Procyon, and Betelgeuse — three of the brightest stars in the sky. The unicorn's own stars are all 4th magnitude or fainter.

steps.title: Beta Monocerotis
steps.caption: Beta Monocerotis is one of the finest triple star systems in the sky — three blue-white components resolvable in any small telescope. The Christmas Tree Cluster (NGC 2264) and the spectacular Cone Nebula appear near the unicorn's head.

steps.title: The Rosette Nebula
steps.caption: The Rosette Nebula (NGC 2237) surrounds the open cluster NGC 2244 in northern Monoceros. In long-exposure photographs it appears as a full-moon-sized glowing ring about 5,200 light-years away; the central cluster is visible in binoculars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Musca — the Fly, a small compact southern constellation lying just south of the Southern Cross.

steps.title: Just south of Crux
steps.caption: Find the Southern Cross — its bottom star is Acrux — then look about 5–8° further south. The compact diamond of Musca stars will be there.

steps.title: A compact diamond
steps.caption: Alpha Muscae is a blue giant and the brightest of the diamond's western stars. Beta Muscae is a visual binary of two blue-white stars. Together they define the western edge, which spans only about 6°. All four main stars fit in a single binocular field.

steps.title: The southern fly
steps.caption: Musca was originally called Musca Australis (Southern Fly) to distinguish it from a former northern fly asterism in Aries, long since abolished. It contains the globular cluster NGC 4372, visible in binoculars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Norma — the Carpenter's Square, a small faint constellation embedded in the rich southern Milky Way between Scorpius and Centaurus.

steps.title: Between Scorpius and Centaurus
steps.caption: Norma lies just northwest of the tail of Scorpius. Find Shaula at the scorpion's stinger, then look about 12° to the west-northwest. The region is embedded in a spectacular stretch of the southern Milky Way — no star in Norma is brighter than magnitude 4 after boundary revisions reassigned its original brightest stars to Scorpius.

steps.title: Gamma-2 Normae
steps.caption: Gamma-2 Normae is the brightest remaining star after the IAU boundary revision. Epsilon Normae and other 4th-magnitude stars complete the right-angle shape spanning about 8°. Despite the faintness of individual stars, the region is a spectacular Milky Way star field rich in open clusters.

steps.title: The Norma Star Cloud
steps.caption: Norma sits atop the brilliant Norma Star Cloud, one of the brightest sections of the southern Milky Way. The open cluster NGC 6067 is a rich binocular cluster of about 100 stars within the constellation. The Norma Galaxy Cluster — one of the most massive nearby clusters, partially hidden by the Milky Way — also lies within Norma's boundaries.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Octans — the Octant, the constellation that contains the south celestial pole, though it holds no bright star near that pole unlike the northern sky's convenient Polaris.

steps.title: Finding the south celestial pole
steps.caption: The south celestial pole lies within Octans, but Sigma Octantis — the nearest naked-eye star to it — is only magnitude 5.4 and barely visible under ideal conditions. The practical way to find the pole is to extend the long axis of Crux about 4.5 cross-lengths past Acrux, or bisect the line between the Southern Pointers perpendicularly.

steps.title: Nu Octantis
steps.caption: Nu Octantis is the constellation's brightest star — an orange giant — but it lies far from the pole itself. The three main stars form a right triangle spread across about 20°. Sigma Octantis, the dim pole star at magnitude 5.4, is less than 1° from the south celestial pole.

steps.title: No southern Polaris
steps.caption: Octans was named by Lacaille after the reflecting octant — a forerunner of the sextant. Unlike Polaris (less than 1° from the north pole and easy to see), the south pole is marked only by dim Sigma Octantis at magnitude 5.4 — invisible from light-polluted sites. The south pole will gain a brighter pole star as precession shifts the axis, but not for thousands of years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Ophiuchus — the Serpent Bearer, a large summer constellation that straddles the celestial equator between Scorpius and Hercules, and notably through which the ecliptic also passes.

steps.title: Look north of Scorpius in summer
steps.caption: In June and July, Ophiuchus is due south at midnight for northern observers. Start at brilliant red Antares in Scorpius and move north about 23°. Rasalhague, Ophiuchus's brightest star, is a clear beacon at that point. The large hexagonal outline of the constellation spans about 20° from side to side.

steps.title: Find Rasalhague
steps.caption: Rasalhague marks the serpent bearer's head — a blue-white star about 47 light-years away. It is easy to spot as the brightest star in the region north of Scorpius and south of the Keystone of Hercules.

steps.title: The serpent bearer's extent
steps.caption: The constellation's hexagonal outline encloses a large area, with Sabik marking the lower center of the figure. The ecliptic passes through the southern part of Ophiuchus — more so than through Scorpius — making it technically the 'thirteenth zodiac constellation.' The star fields along the Ophiuchus–Scorpius border are rich with globular clusters and nebulae.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Orion — arguably the most recognizable constellation in the sky, visible from every inhabited place on Earth.

steps.title: Face south in winter
steps.caption: Look to the southern sky on a winter evening. In the northern hemisphere Orion rides high in the south; in the southern hemisphere he's high in the north. Best seen January through March.

steps.title: Three stars in a row
steps.caption: The Belt is unmistakable — three bright stars of nearly equal magnitude in a perfectly straight row. Nothing else in the sky looks quite like it. Alnitak, Alnilam and Mintaka span just three degrees, yet stand out from dozens of degrees away.

steps.title: The Hunter
steps.caption: With the Belt found, the rest of the Hunter falls into place. At the upper-left shoulder glows Betelgeuse (α Ori, mag 0.4) — a red supergiant nearing the end of its life. At the lower-right foot blazes Rigel (β Ori, mag 0.1) — brilliant blue-white, one of the most luminous stars in the galaxy. The colour contrast is visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: A compass for winter
steps.caption: The Belt is the most useful signpost in the winter sky. Follow it to the right (northwest) and you reach the Pleiades and Aldebaran, the red eye of Taurus. Follow it to the left (southeast) and you reach Sirius — the brightest star in the entire night sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Pavo the Peacock contains Peacock (Alpha Pavonis, mag 1.9), one of the brightest southern stars. Start by getting your bearings in the southern sky.

steps.title: Find Peacock
steps.caption: Peacock sits about 20° south-southeast of Alnair in Grus. Look for the isolated bright blue-white star in the southern sky.

steps.title: Pavo's kite shape
steps.caption: From Peacock (Alpha), look for Beta and Delta Pavonis which form a modest kite or fan shape to the south. Beta and Delta are close together near dec −66°.

steps.title: Pavo's extent
steps.caption: The constellation spans from Peacock in the north to its southern stars near dec −66°. It is best seen July–September from southern latitudes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Pegasus — the Winged Horse, whose four bright stars form the Great Square of Pegasus, one of the most recognizable asterisms of the autumn sky.

steps.title: Look high in the autumn sky
steps.caption: In September and October, the Great Square of Pegasus passes nearly overhead at midnight for mid-northern observers. Each side of the Square is about 15° long, and the interior is strikingly empty of stars — this very emptiness makes it a sky transparency test. From the Summer Triangle's Altair, move north-northeast about 50° to find the Square.

steps.title: The Great Square
steps.caption: The four corners of the Square are Markab (Alpha Peg, mag 2.5), Scheat (Beta Peg, mag 2.4), Algenib (Gamma Peg, mag 2.8), and Alpheratz (shared with Andromeda, mag 2.1). Scheat is a red giant variable, fluctuating between magnitudes 2.4 and 2.8. The northeast corner (Alpheratz) is technically the starting point of Andromeda.

steps.title: Pegasus beyond the Square
steps.caption: South and west of the Square, fainter stars trace the horse's neck and head, ending at Enif (Epsilon Pegasi, mag 2.4), a bright orange supergiant about 690 light-years away. The Great Square is also used as a pointer: extend the western side southward about 45° and you land on Fomalhaut, the lonely bright star of Piscis Austrinus.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Perseus — the Hero, sitting in the winter Milky Way between Cassiopeia and Taurus, home to the famous variable star Algol and a spectacular double star cluster.

steps.title: Look south of Cassiopeia in autumn
steps.caption: In November and December, Perseus is high overhead for northern observers, sitting directly below the W of Cassiopeia. Star-hop from Cassiopeia's brightest star southward about 25° to reach Mirfak. Perseus stretches between Cassiopeia above and Taurus below, rich in Milky Way star fields.

steps.title: Find Mirfak
steps.caption: Mirfak (Alpha Persei, mag 1.8) is the brightest star and sits at the center of a loose association of young stars — the Alpha Persei Cluster (Melotte 20) — visible in binoculars as a beautiful spread of blue-white stars. Mirfak is a yellow-white supergiant about 510 light-years away, intrinsically over 5,000 times more luminous than the Sun.

steps.title: Algol and the Double Cluster
steps.caption: Algol (Beta Persei, mag normally 2.1) is the 'Demon Star' — an eclipsing binary that dims to magnitude 3.4 for about 10 hours every 2.87 days when its fainter companion passes in front. The famous Double Cluster (NGC 869 and 884) lies between Perseus and Cassiopeia, a stunning naked-eye and binocular object — two rich clusters separated by less than half a degree.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Phoenix contains Ankaa (Alpha Phoenicis, mag 2.4), a useful southern hemisphere guide star in a sparse region between Fomalhaut and Achernar.

steps.title: Find Ankaa
steps.caption: Find Ankaa about 30° south of Fomalhaut (the bright Lonely Star of Autumn). It is the brightest star in this area of sky.

steps.title: Phoenix's Y shape
steps.caption: From Ankaa at the southern base, the constellation extends north as a rough Y or bird shape. Beta and Gamma Phoenix are to the northeast.

steps.title: Phoenix's full extent
steps.caption: Phoenix spans a wide area south of the celestial equator. It is best seen October–December from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Pictor the Painter's Easel lies between brilliant Canopus and Achernar in the deep southern sky. Its brightest star, Alpha Pictoris (mag 3.2), is barely conspicuous without context.

steps.title: Find Pictor's region
